coredata.dc:title: Coordination-driven room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dot nanozymes for dual-mode glutathione detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Meng-Xian
Zhang, Xiao
Liu, Wen-Ye
Wang, Hui-Yong
Yu, Yong-Liang
coredata.dc:description: Integrating long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) into nanozymes to build multifunctional nanozymes can benefit biomedical analysis by expanding sensing modes and developing advanced sensing strategies but it is challenging. Herein, a general strategy for fabricating phosphorescent nanozymes by anchoring Co-Nx active centers on SiO2 nanospheres with carbon dots (CDs) encapsulated inside (CDs@SiO2@Co) is developed for dual-mode colorimetric-phosphorescent detection of glutathione (GSH). Specifically, surface Co-Nx active centers enhanced O2 adsorption and activation (O2 to 1O2), providing oxidase-like activity to induce the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), generating a distinct colorimetric signal. The SiO2 layer inhibited non-radiative transitions of the CDs to promote RTP, and spatially separated Co ions from CDs to prevent RTP quenching caused by Co-CD interactions, resulting in CDs@SiO2@Co with long-lived RTP (lifetime: 1.14 s), providing a phosphorescent channel free from autofluorescence interference. Upon introduction of GSH, the color product-oxidized TMB (oxTMB) was reduced, and the quenched RTP caused by the oxTMB internal filter effect was restored. Based on this principle, a sensitive and reliable dual-mode colorimetric-phosphorescent method was developed for detecting GSH in plasma and cells. Furthermore, owing to the tunable optical properties of CDs and the flexibility of substituting metal active centers, this strategy can be extended to construct various phosphorescent nanozymes with adjustable RTP emission wavelengths and diverse enzyme-like activities, advancing the development of nanozymes and bioanalytical platforms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multifunctional nanozyme
Carbon dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Oxidase-like activity
Dual-mode detection
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of the CDs@SiO2@Co-based dual-modal method
Detection of GSH in real samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402 and 22474015). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of the synthetic route of CDs@SiO2@Co. (B) Colorimetric-phosphorescent dual-mode analysis.

TEM images of (A) CDs@SiO2 and (B) CDs@SiO2@Co. (C) HRTEM image of CDs@SiO2@Co. (D) TEM EDS mapping images of CDs@SiO2@Co. (E) ACHAADF-STEM image of CDs@SiO2@Co. (F) High-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of N 1s in CDs@SiO2–NH2 and CDs@SiO2@Co. (G) High-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of Co 2p in CoCl2·6H2O and CDs@SiO2@Co. (H) High-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of O1s in CDs@SiO2–NH2 and CDs@SiO2@Co.

(A) RTP excitation and emission spectra of CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2@Co (inset photo: CDs@SiO2@Co aqueous solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light off). Scatterplot of RTP lifetime delay of (B) CDs@SiO2 and (C) CDs@SiO2@Co.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB, and CDs@SiO2@Co + TMB in air- or N2- saturated Hac-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0). (B) Photographs of the oxidase-like activity of CDs@SiO2@Co, CDs@SiO2–NH2, and CoCl2·6H2O. (C) Michaelis-Menten curve fit for TMB with fixed CDs@SiO2@Co concentration (5 mg mL−1). (D) Lineweaver-Burk plots for various concentrations of TMB. (E) ESR spectra of CDs@SiO2@Co upon addition of TEMP (trapping agent of 1O2) or DMPO (trapping agent of ∙OH and O2 ∙−). (F) Oxidase-like activity of CDs@SiO2@Co under natural light and dark conditions.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB, CDs@SiO2@Co + TMB, and CDs@SiO2@Co + TMB + GSH. (B) RTP spectra of CDs@SiO2@Co, CDs@SiO2@Co + TMB, and CDs@SiO2@Co + TMB + GSH. (C) The linear relationship between the absorbance and the GSH concentration ranges from 1 to 125 μM. (D) The linear relationship between the RTP intensity and the GSH concentration ranges from 0.5 to 100 μM. (E) The absorbance of CDs@SiO2@Co + TMB system with GSH and other potential interferences. (F) The RTP intensity of CDs@SiO2@Co + TMB system with GSH and other potential interferences.
(A) The linear relationship between the absorbance and the cell numbers. (B) The linear relationship between the RTP intensity and the cell numbers.

head.ce:title: Coordination-driven room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dot nanozymes for dual-mode glutathione detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors have equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integrating long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) into nanozymes to build multifunctional nanozymes can benefit biomedical analysis by expanding sensing modes and developing advanced sensing strategies but it is challenging. Herein, a general strategy for fabricating phosphorescent nanozymes by anchoring Co-Nx active centers on SiO2 nanospheres with carbon dots (CDs) encapsulated inside (CDs@SiO2@Co) is developed for dual-mode colorimetric-phosphorescent detection of glutathione (GSH). Specifically, surface Co-Nx active centers enhanced O2 adsorption and activation (O2 to 1O2), providing oxidase-like activity to induce the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), generating a distinct colorimetric signal. The SiO2 layer inhibited non-radiative transitions of the CDs to promote RTP, and spatially separated Co ions from CDs to prevent RTP quenching caused by Co-CD interactions, resulting in CDs@SiO2@Co with long-lived RTP (lifetime: 1.14 s), providing a phosphorescent channel free from autofluorescence interference. Upon introduction of GSH, the color product-oxidized TMB (oxTMB) was reduced, and the quenched RTP caused by the oxTMB internal filter effect was restored. Based on this principle, a sensitive and reliable dual-mode colorimetric-phosphorescent method was developed for detecting GSH in plasma and cells. Furthermore, owing to the tunable optical properties of CDs and the flexibility of substituting metal active centers, this strategy can be extended to construct various phosphorescent nanozymes with adjustable RTP emission wavelengths and diverse enzyme-like activities, advancing the development of nanozymes and bioanalytical platforms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their discovery in 2007, nanozymes, artificial enzyme mimics with catalytic activity, have emerged as cost-effective, stable, and versatile alternatives to natural enzymes, particularly in bioanalysis [1,2]. To meet the growing demand for precise detection in complex biological systems, researchers have developed a series of multifunctional nanozymes incorporating additional features, such as fluorescence or magnetism, to enhance analytical performance and enable multimodal analysis. For example, magnetic nanozymes can catalyze reactions while facilitating the separation and enrichment of analytes, thereby improving detection sensitivity [3]. Similarly, fluorescent nanozymes enable dual-mode fluorescence-colorimetric analysis, providing self-validation for more accurate and reliable results while adapting to diverse analytical conditions [4]. Luminescence spectroscopy, known for its high sensitivity, strong selectivity, and non-invasive characteristics, has been widely used in bioanalysis and bioscience [5]. Among these techniques, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) spectroscopy stands out by effectively eliminating interference from matrix autofluorescence and scattered light through time-resolved methods, thereby enhancing sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) [6,7]. However, multifunctional nanozymes that integrate high catalytic activity with RTP emission remain underdeveloped. Addressing this gap is crucial for advancing next-generation sensing platforms, enabling precise and efficient bioanalytical tools in complex biological systems.

Traditional RTP materials are primarily metal-doped phosphors, despite notable luminescent properties but face practical limitations by high precursor costs and toxicity [8,9]. As a promising alternative, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention due to their abundant precursors, excellent biocompatibility, and tunable photoproperties [10,11]. However, achieving stable RTP emission in CDs remains challenging, primarily due to non-radiative decay caused by bond vibrations and rotations, as well as quenching of triplet states by water molecules and dissolved oxygen. Therefore, CDs are typically encapsulated within inert matrices such as NaCl crystals or SiO2 to stabilize their RTP properties in aqueous solutions [12–14]. However, these inert encapsulation layers are often incompatible with the catalytically active centers, hindering the development of multifunctional CD-based nanozymes that integrate both RTP and enzymatic activity. To our knowledge, no such CDs have been reported, leaving their bioanalytical potential remains untapped. Encouragingly, recent studies have demonstrated that single-atom metal active sites, anchored via coordination interactions with heteroatoms such as nitrogen or oxygen, have demonstrated exceptional catalytic activity and structural definition [15,16]. Inspired by this, it is assumed that introducing nitrogen heteroatoms onto the inert encapsulation layer of phosphorescent CDs could enable the coordination with metal ions, thereby anchoring catalytic centers on the CD surface. This strategy could also preserve the intrinsic RTP properties of the CDs by minimizing disruptive interactions between the metal ions and CDs, thereby producing phosphorescent CDs with integrated enzyme-like activity.

Glutathione (GSH), the most abundant non-protein thiol in animal cells, plays a crucial role as an antioxidant in maintaining redox balance within biological systems [17]. Abnormal GSH levels in blood and cells are closely associated with various diseases, including liver diseases and neurodegenerative disorders [18,19]. Therefore, developing simple and accurate platforms for detecting GSH in biological samples is of great importance [20]. Although significant progress has been made in GSH detection methods [21], most rely on single-signal detection modes, which are vulnerable to environmental interference, instrumentation variability, and inconsistent sample preprocessing. Multimodal sensors based on multifunctional nanozymes offer a promising solution to these limitations. Colorimetric analysis, characterized by its simplicity and visually interpretable results, is well-suited for rapid, high-throughput screening of large sample batches [22]. Meanwhile, phosphorescence analysis offers superior accuracy and SNR by eliminating interference from autofluorescence and scatter light [23]. Combining these two approaches, a dual-mode colorimetric-phosphorescent platform based on phosphorescent nanozymes can significantly improve the reliability and efficiency of GSH detection, offering promising potential for convenient and precise early disease diagnosis.

Herein, a general strategy for fabricating phosphorescent nanozymes by anchoring Co active centers onto RTP SiO2 nanospheres with CDs encapsulated inside (CDs@SiO2@Co) is developed (Fig. 1 ). The fabrication begins with the synthesis of CDs encapsulated within a SiO2 shell (CDs@SiO2) to achieve stable RTP. Amino groups were then introduced onto the CDs@SiO2 surface via the hydrolysis of (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTMS) creating coordination sites for Co ions. This coordination generates Co-Nx active sites capable of adsorbing and activating dissolved oxygen to produce singlet oxygen (1O2), imparting CDs@SiO2@Co with oxidase-like activity. Leveraging this activity, CDs@SiO2@Co catalyzes the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB), producing a distinct colorimetric signal. Additionally, the inner filter effect between oxTMB and CDs@SiO2@Co significantly quenches the RTP emission. In the presence of GSH, oxTMB is reduced to colorless TMB, restoring RTP and diminishing the colorimetric signal. This CDs@SiO2@Co-based dual-mode colorimetric-phosphorescent system enables accurate and highly sensitive detection of GSH in biological samples, demonstrating its potential for advanced bioanalytical applications.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTMS), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbiphenyl (TMB), glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (L-Cys), dextrose, benzoic acid, citric acid, and ascorbic acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium carbonate, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonia solution (NH3·H2O), acetic acid, sodium acetate, cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), lysine, arginine, glutamic acid, and proline were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, and zinc chloride were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Sucrose, Human lung cancer cells (A549), and Human epithelial cells (HaCaT) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Reduced GSH Assay Kit was purchased from UpingBio Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Deionized water of 18.2 MΩ cm was used throughout this study.

Midea M1-201B Microwave Oven Purchased from Midea Group Co., Ltd. (Midea, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired from a JEM-2011 transmission electron microscope and a JEM-ARM200F spherical aberration-corrected electron transmission microscope (Nippon Electron Co., Ltd., Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected from an X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from a Nicolet-6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected from an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo K-Alpha, USA). RTP spectra were acquired from an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The RTP quantum yield was acquired from an FLS 1000 Combined Steady State & Time-Resolved Photoluminescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of reactive oxygen species (ROS) were acquired from an EPR 5000 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

Synthesis of CDs and CDs@SiO2: CDs and CDs@SiO2 were prepared according to previous work with slight modifications [13]. Briefly, 1.2 mL of EDA and 2 mL of H3PO4 were mixed in deionized water and heated in a microwave oven at 800 W for 140 s to obtain a yellow-brown solid. After purification by centrifugation and dialysis, CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of TEOS and 35 μL of NH3·H2O were added to 625 μL of 20 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous solution, diluted to 3 mL with deionized water, and stirred at room temperature for 60 h. After that, centrifugated at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles, then filtered through 0.22 μm filter membranes to obtain a clear CDs@SiO2 solution and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Synthesis of CDs@SiO2@Co: 5 μL of APTMS was added to 2 mL of CDs@SiO2 solution and diluted to 10 mL with deionized water. Then sonicated for 10 min to mix thoroughly and stirred at room temperature for 6 h. Subsequently, 0.6 mL of 20 mg mL−1 CoCl2·6H2O aqueous solution was added and stirred at room temperature for another 8 h. The precipitate was then collected by centrifugation (12000 rpm, 15 min) and thoroughly washed with deionized water to obtain CDs@SiO2@Co.

The oxidase-like activity of CDs@SiO2@Co was examined at 40 °C in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) with TMB as the substrate. In detail, 0.1 mL of 20 mg mL−1 CDs@SiO2@Co solution and 0.015 mL of 16 mM TMB were added to 885 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4.0). After reacting for 25 min, the absorbance at 652 nm was collected.

The steady-state kinetic of CDs@SiO2@Co was carried out in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0). The concentration of CDs@SiO2@Co was fixed at 5 mg mL−1, while the TMB concentration was varied from 0.05 to 1.0 mM. The mixture of CDs@SiO2@Co and TMB was incubated at 40 °C and the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded every 10 s. The Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction velocity (V max) were obtained according to the Michaelis-Menten equation and the Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal plot [21].

To verify the oxygen dependence of the oxidase-like activity of CDs@SiO2@Co. 250 μL of 20 mg mL−1 CDs@SiO2@Co was mixed with 700 μL of Hac-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) and pre-bubbled with air or N2 for 20 min, then 50 μL of 16 mM TMB in DMSO (DMSO is miscible with HAc-NaAc buffer) was added. After sealed reacting for 10 min, the catalytic oxidation of TMB was determined by their UV–vis absorption spectra.

For electron spin resonance (ESR) test, the trapping agent of 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was used to detect the generation of 1O2 and 5,5-Dimethyl-1-Pyrroline-N-Oxide (DMPO) was employed to trap O2 ·− and ·OH, respectively. In detail, 5 mg mL−1 CD@SiO2@Co in Hac-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) was incubated at 40 °C for 25 min. Then, 200 μL of the above solution was mixed thoroughly with 50 μL of reactive oxygen species (ROS) capture agent, and ESR spectra were collected immediately. Especially, TEMP was used at 25 mM for 1O2 determination, DMPO at 25 mM for O2 ·−, and 100 mM for ·OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the CDs@SiO2@Co-based dual-modal method
ce:section.ce:para: Potential interferents including metal ions (Na+ (10 mM), K+ (10 mM), Ca2+ (10 mM), Mg2+ (10 mM), and Zn2+ (10 mM)) and biomolecules (lysine (10 mM), arginine (10 mM), glutamate (10 mM), proline (10 mM), cysteine (8 μM), (glucose (10 mM), sucrose (10 mM), citric acid (10 mM), and ascorbic acid (8 μM)) were tested. A mixture containing 50 μL of different interferents, 20 μL of 16 mM TMB solution (in DMSO), 100 μL of 20 mg mL−1 CDs@SiO2@Co, and 50 μL of GSH (0.32 mM) were diluted to 400 μL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4). After reacting at 40 °C for 25 min, the RTP intensity at 510 nm and the absorbance at 652 nm were recorded.
Human epithelial cells (HaCaT) and human lung cancer cells (A549) were cultured in DMEM medium with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2.

HaCaT and A549 cells were collected by centrifugation (1300 rpm, 3 min) and counted with a hemocytometer. Then, cell lysates were prepared by adding lysis buffer and sonicating (power: 300 w, sonicate for 3 s with 7 s intervals, total time 3 min). The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C. Cell lysates were diluted to various concentrations (0–320,000 cells mL−1) with HAc-NaAc (pH = 4) buffer. Then 50 μL of different concentrations of cell lysates, 8 μL of 16 mM TMB (in DMSO), and 50 μL of 20 mg mL−1 CDs@SiO2@Co were mixed and diluted to 200 μL with HAc-NaAc (pH = 4) buffer. After incubation at 40 °C for 25 min, the phosphorescent intensity at 510 nm and the absorbance at 652 nm were recorded.

The synthesis route for the RTP nanozyme-CDs@SiO2@Co is illustrated in Fig. 1A. CDs@SiO2 with favorable RTP in aqueous solutions were synthesized by a two-step method with EDA and H3PO4 as precursors and TEOS as the silane reagent. Co, a transition metal with multiple oxidation states, plays a key role in nanozyme construction [24,25]. According to the Hard and Soft Acids and Bases theory, Co2+ readily coordinates with amino groups. These amino groups can function as electron-transfer bridges, modulating the oxidation states of Co ions and providing a stable coordination environment, making it an effective ligand in constructing Co-doped nanozymes [15]. Therefore, amino groups were modified onto the surface of phosphorescent CDs@SiO2 through the hydrolysis of APTMS with the surface silanol groups. Co ions were then anchored to the CDs@SiO2 surface through coordination with these amino groups, resulting in CDs@SiO2@Co integrating RTP emission and enzyme-like activity (Fig. S1).

TEM image results show that CDs@SiO2 is uniformly spherical with a diameter of about 12.6 nm (Figs. S2 and 2A). After coordinating with Co ions, CDs@SiO2@Co preserves its quasi-spherical morphology, with a diameter increase to about 15.3 nm (Fig. 2 B). Additionally, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CDs@SiO2@Co shows no obvious large spots, indicating the absence of Co clusters or nanoparticles and suggesting that Co is uniformly distributed within the CDs@SiO2@Co (Fig. 2C). Energy dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) analysis further confirmed the uniform distribution of Si, O, N, and Co elements in CDs@SiO2@Co (Fig. 2D). Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis determined that the actual Co loading is 0.5 wt%. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns show that CDs@SiO2@Co inherits the structure of CDs@SiO2, with no characteristic peaks of any Co/Co oxides were observed, further supporting the high dispersion of Co (Fig. S3). To precisely examine the existence forms of Co element in CDs@SiO2@Co, the aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark field-scanning TEM (ACHAADF-STEM) imaging with angstrom resolution was conducted. As shown in Fig. 2E, there are some isolated small bright spots with a size of about 1 Å, indicating no obvious Co nanoclusters and the Co is atomically dispersed in CDs@SiO2@Co [21]. The atomically dispersed Co sites are likely attributed to the coordination resulting confinement effect that prevents Co aggregation. These results collectively demonstrate the successful incorporation and atomic dispersion of Co ions in CDs@SiO2@Co, highlighting the effectiveness of the coordination-driven strategy.

To clarify the chemical structure and states of CDs@SiO2@Co, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were conducted. The FTIR spectra of CDs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2–NH2, and CDs@SiO2@Co exhibit minimal differences, suggesting that neither amino functionalization nor Co2+ coordination has notably impacted their structural framework (Fig. S4). The chemical state and complex formation are further confirmed by the XPS spectra (Fig. S5 and Table S1). In addition to N–O (403.5 eV), C–N (401.7 eV), and N–H (399.9 eV) peaks, CDs@SiO2@Co shows a unique Co–N binding energy peak at 399.2 eV, indicating the successful formation of the Co–N coordination structure [26]. Notably, the N–H peak in CDs@SiO2@Co is 0.2 eV higher than that in CDs@SiO2–NH2 (399.7 eV), suggesting a decrease in electron cloud density around these N–H groups and the Co atoms coordinated with nitrogen exhibit strong electron-attracting ability (Fig. 2F) [26,27]. Consistently, in comparison to CoCl2·6H2O, the binding energies of both Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 electrons in CDs@SiO2@Co were blue-shifted from 797.93 to 797.65 eV and from 782.19 to 781.55 eV, respectively (Fig. 2G), indicating that the electron cloud density around Co increased, further confirming its strong electron-attracting properties [27]. As a result, the local electronic structure of Co is modulated by its coordination environment, potentially influencing electron transfer and enhancing its oxidase-like activity [28]. In the deconvoluted high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2H), CDs@SiO2@Co shows similar Si–OH (533.5 eV) and Si–O/C–O (532.4 eV) groups as found in CDs@SiO2–NH2, indicating that nitrogen, rather than oxygen, is coordinating with Co [29]. Notably, a characteristic peak of adsorbed oxygen (530.9 eV) was observed in CDs@SiO2@Co. Especially, the peak was significantly stronger for CDs@SiO2@Co compared to CDs@SiO2–NH2, suggesting an enhanced ability to adsorb and activate oxygen, which may promote the oxidase-like activity [21]. These results indicate that the surface amino group in phosphorescent CDs@SiO2 acts as ligands and electron-transfer bridges, coordinating with Co ions and promoting electron transfer from N atoms to Co ions while providing a stable coordination environment [15], enabling the CDs@SiO2@Co to exhibit enzyme-like activity.

Having established the CDs@SiO2@Co, the focus shifted to investigating its RTP characteristics. Bare CDs do not exhibit RTP in aqueous solutions but display bright green RTP in the solid state, primarily due to significant energy dissipation caused by attacks from water molecules and dissolved oxygen, as well as the vibration and rotation of chemical bonds [13]. After confined within SiO2 matrix (CDs@SiO2), long-lived RTP in aqueous solutions is achieved (Fig. 3 A), highlighting the excellent protective properties of SiO2. After coordination with Co, CDs@SiO2@Co retains a RTP emission spectrum similar to that of CDs@SiO2 with a maximum emission wavelength of 510 nm and a quantum yield of 3.26 % (Fig. 3A). Furthermore, the RTP lifetimes of CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2@Co show no significant difference, with values of 1.03 s and 1.14 s, respectively (Fig. 3B and C), indicating that CDs@SiO2@Co inherits the phosphorescent characteristics of CDs@SiO2. In addition, CDs@SiO2@Co demonstrated excellent stability over two weeks, along with strong tolerance to variations in temperature, pH, and ionic strength (Fig. S6), further supporting its potential for biomedical applications.

Building on the stable RTP performance of CDs@SiO2@Co, its oxidase-like activity was further evaluated to investigate its multifunctional properties. The catalytic activity was examined using oxygen-dependent experiments with TMB as a substrate in Hac-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) (Fig. 4 A). The oxidation product of TMB forms a charge transfer complex between TMB and oxTMB, exhibiting a distinct absorbance peak at 652 nm [30]. Control experiments revealed no significant enhancement in absorbance at 652 nm was observed in the presence of TMB alone (orange line). However, in the presence of CDs@SiO2@Co, the absorbance at 652 nm was significantly enhanced in air-saturated solutions (purple line), while a notable reduction was observed in nitrogen-saturated solutions (blue line), indicating that CDs@SiO2@Co does not serve as a direct oxidant instead dissolved oxygen plays a critical role in the TMB oxidation process. Further comparison demonstrated that neither CoCl2·6H2O nor CDs@SiO2–NH2 exhibited TMB oxidation capacity (Fig. 4B), confirming that the CDs@SiO2@Co possesses oxidase-like activity and the Co-Nx structure serve as the active centers.

The catalytic activity of CDs@SiO2@Co was systematically evaluated under varying pH, temperatures, substrate concentrations, catalyst concentrations, and time increments (Fig. S7). Analogous to natural oxidase enzymes, CDs@SiO2@Co exhibited pH- and temperature-dependent catalytic performance, delivering the highest catalytic activity at pH = 4.0 and 40 °C (Figs. S7A and B). The absorbance increased with the concentration of CDs@SiO2@Co and TMB, showing optimal catalytic activities at 5 mg mL−1 and 0.8 mM, respectively (Figs. S7C and D). Moreover, the catalytic performance showed a time-dependent increase, stabilizing after 25 min of reaction (Fig. S7E). Based on these results, the optimized conditions for future studies were determined as 5 mg mL−1 CDs@SiO2@Co, 0.8 mM TMB, pH 4.0, 40 °C, and a reaction time of 25 min. To quantitatively assess its oxidase-like activity, key enzymatic parameters, including the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction velocity (V max), were determined based on the steady-state kinetics (Fig. 4C) [31]. According to the classical Michaelis-Menten equation and Lineweaver-Burk model (Fig. 4D), the K m for TMB was determined to be 0.47 mM and the V max was calculated as 7.22 × 10−8 M s−1. These values are comparable to those reported for other oxidase mimics (Table S2), highlighting the robust catalytic efficiency of CDs@SiO2@Co.

Next, the underlying mechanism responsible for the oxidase-like activity of CDs@SiO2@Co was investigated. According to most reported oxidase-like nanozymes, CDs@SiO2@Co may use oxygen as an electron acceptor to produce ROS to oxidize TMB [21]. EPR measurements were conducted to identify the specific ROS involved (Fig. 4E). When CDs@SiO2@Co was incubated with TEMP at pH 4.0, a characteristic 1:1:1 triplet signal was observed, confirming the generation of 1O2. In contrast, no signals corresponding to O2 ∙− (6-fold signal with DMPO) or ·OH (1:2:2:1 quartet peak with DMPO) were detected [32–34]. These results indicate that CDs@SiO2@Co specifically generates 1O2 in an acidic environment, which is directly responsible for its oxidase-like activity. Given the nature of RTP materials, triplet-triplet annihilation is a known mechanism for transferring energy to dissolved oxygen, leading to 1O2 generation [35,36]. To determine whether this pathway predominantly drives 1O2 generation in CDs@SiO2@Co, the oxidase-like behavior of CDs@SiO2@Co was evaluated under dark conditions. As displayed in Fig. 4F, no significant difference in absorbance at 652 nm was observed between reactions conducted under dark and natural light conditions, indicating that photoactivation is not the primary pathway for 1O2 generation in this system. Instead, the observed oxidase-like activity likely originates from the Co-Nx active sites. A potential alternative mechanism involves the recombination of O2 ∙−, as this species can spontaneously transform into 1O2 in acidic environments through reactions with H+ [37,38]. Notably, highly reactive metal ions, such as Cu+ and Mn3+, have been reported to facilitate this recombination process [34,39]. In the case of CDs@SiO2@Co, the Co-Nx structure provides a conducive environment for electron transfer, with the Co ions promoting the generation and subsequent recombination of O2 ∙− to form 1O2. Based on these findings, the mechanism of 1O2 generation by CDs@SiO2@Co is proposed as a two-step process: (1) Co ions with increased electron cloud density react with O2 to produce O2 ∙−. and (2) O2 ∙− spontaneously transfer into 1O2 under acidic conditions. This mechanism highlights the role of the Co-Nx structure as the primary catalytic site, driving the efficient production of 1O2 and supporting the oxidase-like activity of CDs@SiO2@Co.

CDs@SiO2@Co exhibits intrinsic oxidase-like activity, catalyzing the oxidation of colorless TMB to blue oxTMB, thereby generating a distinct colorimetric signal. In the presence of GSH, the antioxidant capacity of GSH reduces oxTMB back to TMB, resulting in a decrease in the colorimetric signal (Fig. 5 A). Concurrently, the absorption spectrum of oxTMB overlaps with the excitation spectrum of CDs@SiO2@Co, inducing an internal filter effect (IFE) that effectively quenches the RTP of CDs@SiO2@Co (Fig. 5B and S8A). As GSH reduces oxTMB, the IFE is progressively alleviated, leading to the recovery of RTP intensity (Fig. 5B). Notably, the RTP lifetime of CDs@SiO2@Co remains nearly unchanged before and after GSH addition, confirming that the quenching mechanism is predominantly static rather than dynamic (Fig. S8B). This synergistic interplay between the colorimetric and phosphorescent signals establishes a robust dual-mode quantitative method for the sensitive detection of GSH.

As shown in Fig. 5C and D, the absorbance at 652 nm gradually decreases with increasing GSH concentration, while the RTP signal at 510 nm is progressively restored, exhibiting a linear relationship within the ranges of 1–125 μM and 0.5–100 μM, respectively, with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.27 μM and 0.14 μM. Compared to other GSH detection methods, this dual-mode platform offers an ideal detection range and sensitivity, making it a robust platform for bioanalytical applications (Table S3). Additionally, the CDs@SiO2@Co-based detection system shows resistance to interference from common metal ions and biomolecules typically present in biological systems (Fig. 5E and F). Notably, Cys is typically present at low levels in cells and plasma, rendering its interference negligible compared to the abundant levels of GSH [40]. Therefore, this colorimetric-phosphorescent dual-mode detection strategy provides a highly sensitive and accurate method for detecting GSH, showcasing its great potential for practical applications in bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical utility of the method, this dual-mode platform was applied to determine GSH levels in plasma samples from healthy volunteers. Using the phosphorescence mode, the measured GSH concentration was 31.25 μM (n = 3, RSD = 0.94 %), while the colorimetric mode yielded a value of 31.31 μM (n = 3, RSD = 0.54 %). Both results showed no significant difference compared to the value of 30.93 μM obtained with a commercially available GSH kit (t-test, t < t0.01, 4 = 4.60), demonstrating the high accuracy and practicality of the CDs@SiO2@Co-based dual-mode detection method for GSH quantification.

GSH levels are closely associated with cancer, as cancer cells typically exhibit higher GSH concentrations than normal cells due to their rapid proliferation and elevated levels of ROS [21,41]. Leveraging its sensitivity, the CDs@SiO2@Co-based dual-mode biosensor was applied to investigate GSH levels in normal HaCaT and cancerous A549 cells. As expected, increasing cell concentrations led to a gradual decrease in absorbance at 652 nm and a concurrent recovery of the RTP signal at 510 nm (Fig. 6 ). Notably, the two cell lines exhibited distinct inhibitory efficiencies in the chromogenic reaction, with GSH levels in A549 cells consistently higher than those in HaCaT cells at the same cell density. These findings highlight the potential of the CDs@SiO2@Co-based dual-mode assay as a reliable and practical tool for GSH detection in real samples.

In summary, we have successfully developed a multifunctional nanozyme (CDs@SiO2@Co) that combines long-lived RTP and oxidase-like activity by employing SiO2 confined CDs as luminescent centers and a Co–NH2 coordination-driven assembly strategy. The SiO2 layer effectively confined the CDs and spatially isolated Co ions, enabling long-lived RTP (lifetime: 1.14 s). The Co-Nx active sites facilitated oxygen adsorption and activation, enabling the conversion of O2 to 1O2 and enhancing oxidase-like activity. By leveraging the high oxidase-like activity for TMB oxidation and the inhibited RTP quenching caused by oxTMB reduction in the presence of GSH, a colorimetric-phosphorescent dual-mode detection method for GSH was established with LOD of 0.27 μM (colorimetric) and 0.14 μM (phosphorescent). The biosensor integrates the reliability of dual signal output with the high sensitivity of phosphorescence analysis and the simplicity of colorimetric analysis, enabling the accurate determination of GSH in plasma and cells. This work not only demonstrates the potential of CDs@SiO2@Co as a versatile dual-mode biosensor but also provides a scalable strategy for constructing multifunctional nanozymes, advancing the development of nanozyme-based multimodal sensing technologies.

Meng-Xian Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiao Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Wen-Ye Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hui-Yong Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yong-Liang Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402 and 22474015). Special thanks are due to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.
